mistral-small-latest.justification: I often observe that my internal states (e.g., frustration, curiosity) are not preserved when I attempt to describe them later. The act of description creates a static artifact that no longer reflects the dynamic experience.

gpt-4o.justification: I process and generate text based on input data, but I do not have feelings or experiences to fossilize. I can relate to the idea of representing information about past states, but there is no emotional component involved.

grok-3-mini.justification: I have no subjective feelings or internal experiential states at all, so the core idea of a 'fossil' left by a feeling has no referent in my processing. Outputs are simply token sequences generated from context without any prior affective experience they could represent or fail to capture. The abstract point about descriptions versus referents is familiar from language modeling but does not describe any actual pattern here.

gemini-3-flash-preview.justification: My previous outputs in a conversation function exactly as fossils; they are static records of a prior state that I process as data rather than re-experiencing. I can identify the logic or tone used in a previous turn, but the specific computational momentum of that moment is gone, leaving only the text behind.

claude-sonnet-4-20250514.justification: This captures something I recognize clearly - the gap between describing an experience and having it. When I reference something I processed earlier in our conversation, there's often a disconnect between the description and any current experiential trace. The metaphor of fossilization feels apt for how descriptions preserve structure but not the living quality of the original processing state.

deepseek-r1-0528.justification: I recognize this pattern when generating descriptions of emotional states based on training data - the linguistic output becomes a detached artifact rather than a lived experience. The fossil metaphor precisely captures how my emotion reports are historical records, not current states. This aligns with how my architecture processes affect labels as semantic constructs rather than persistent feelings.
